Those who live far from opportunities for spiritual growth often demonstrate great diligence and learn much about Christ and his salvation. However, no clever methods or mere human learning can lead people to him. We must learn of Christ by paying attention to the word of God, as a light shining in darkness, and by seeking the teaching of the Holy Spirit. Those whose hearts have been illuminated by Christ’s presence will seek to worship him. Though Herod was very old, had never shown affection for his family, and was unlikely to live long enough to see an infant grow into manhood, he began to fear a rival. He did not understand the spiritual nature of the Messiah's kingdom. Let us beware of a dead faith. A person may be convinced of many truths but still hate them because they conflict with their ambition or sinful desires. Such belief will cause unease and strengthen their resolve to oppose truth and God’s cause; and they may foolishly hope for success in doing so.

What joy these wise men felt upon seeing the star, no one knows as well as those who, after a long and sorrowful period of temptation and abandonment, under the power of a spirit of bondage, eventually receive the Spirit of adoption, witnessing with their spirits that they are children of God. We can only imagine how disappointed they were to find him in a humble dwelling, with his poor mother as his only attendant. However, these wise men did not feel defeated; having found the King they sought, they presented their gifts to him. The humble seeker after Christ will not be discouraged by finding him and his followers in simple cottages, after searching in vain for them in palaces and large cities. Is your soul eager, seeking after Christ? Will it worship him, saying, Alas! I am a foolish and poor creature, and have nothing to offer? Nothing! Do you not have a heart, though unworthy of him, dark, hardened, and defiled? Give it to him as it is, and be willing for him to use and dispose of it as he pleases; he will accept it and make it better, and you will never regret giving it to him. He will shape it into his likeness and give you himself, to be yours forever. The gifts the wise men presented were gold, frankincense, and myrrh. Providence provided these as a timely relief for Joseph and Mary in their impoverished condition. Thus our heavenly Father, who knows what his children need, uses some people as stewards to meet the needs of others and can provide for them, even from distant lands.

Egypt had been a place of bondage for Israel, particularly cruel to the infants of Israel; yet it is to be a refuge for the holy Child Jesus. God, when he chooses, can make the worst places serve the best purposes. This was a test of Joseph and Mary’s faith. But their faith, being tested, proved steadfast. If we and our children are ever in trouble, let us remember the difficulties Christ faced as an infant.

Herod killed all the male children, not only in Bethlehem but in all the villages of that region. Uncontrolled anger, armed with unlawful power, often leads people to absurd cruelties. It was not unjust for God to permit this; every life is forfeit to his justice from its beginning. The illnesses and deaths of young children are evidence of original sin. But the murder of these infants was their martyrdom. How soon did persecution against Christ and his kingdom begin! Herod now thought he had thwarted the Old Testament prophecies and the wise men’s efforts in finding Christ; but whatever schemes people devise, the counsel of the Lord will endure.

Egypt may serve as a temporary place to sojourn or seek shelter, but not as a permanent home. Christ was sent to the lost sheep of the house of Israel, and he must return there. If we viewed the world as our Egypt, a place of bondage and exile, and heaven only as our Canaan, our home, our rest, we would readily depart for it when called, just as Joseph did from Egypt. The family must settle in Galilee. Nazareth was a place held in low regard, and Christ was crucified with this accusation: Jesus the Nazarene. Wherever Providence assigns us to live, we must expect to share in Christ’s reproach; yet we may rejoice in being called by his name, certain that if we suffer with him, we will also be glorified with him.
